Recommendation / Requirement: Declaring a required minimum set of Baseline Attributes necessary to identify Components with sufficient relative uniqueness
Rationale/Context: Minimum attributes needed for unique component identification

Recommendation / Requirement: identifying supplemental, optional Attributes and external elements beyond the baseline set to serve a variety of SBOM applications
Rationale/Context: Optional attributes can support diverse use cases

Recommendation / Requirement: An SBOM’s inventory should be as comprehensive as possible
An SBOM’s inventory should explicitly state where relationships cannot be articulated
a machine-readable format is critical to support data Consumers to manage this for efficiency and expandability
Choosing a specific data format is an important part of this functionality
Another important aspect is the universal naming of Components
As a minimum, the Baseline Attributes are required
Supplemental elements and Attributes can be included to enable SBOM use cases identified later in this document
Code: Allowing Supplemental Data for Use Cases

Recommendation / Requirement: An SBOM system that follows the guidance and framing proposed in this document must support these Baseline Attributes.
An SBOM system or format may support supplemental Attributes.
The Author Name Attribute should name as many participants involved in authoring the SBOM data as possible.
The tool(s) used to create the SBOM can also be declared to assist in SBOM data consumption.
An SBOM must list the entity that prompted the creation of the SBOM.
The Author Name Attribute should include the name of the legal entity and some form of unique identification (e.g., an email address or website) if possible.
If no legal entity name is available, attempt to uniquely identify the SBOM creator along with contact information.
Code: Identify SBOM creator if no entity

Recommendation / Requirement: In addition to listing the entity that prompted the creation of the SBOM, identify tool(s) and version(s) that assisted the Author in the SBOMs creation.
As a minimum expectation, the Timestamp should be consistent across time zones and locales and use a common international format, such as ISO 8601 (e.g., 2024-05-23T13:51:37Z).
This Attribute is optional and considered an aspirational goal.
For each Component in the SBOM, identifying each Attribute should be attempted as indicated in the data maturity levels.
Code: Populate all component attributes adhering to Maturity Levels

Recommendation / Requirement: An SBOM needs to capture transitive, or nested, Supply Chain relationships between Components to the extent that these dependency relationships are known.
SBOMs are expected to identify all static, direct Dependencies of the root or primary Component.
In addition to the direct Dependencies, SBOMs should identify as many levels of subcomponents as possible.
In addition to the direct Dependencies and subcomponents identified in the SBOM, efforts are made to uniquely and unambiguously identify Dependencies that are dynamic and/or remote.
Formats and tooling need to provide the capability to handle multiple names or aliases.
Rationale/Context: Tools must support multiple names or namespace:name constructs

Recommendation / Requirement: As a minimum expectation, the Component name should declare the commonly used public name for the Component.
This Attribute helps to further identify a Component and should be separate from the Component Name.
Recording what is provided from the Supplier accurately is the primary goal.
Semantic versioning is preferred.
If the Component does not have a unique semantic version available to declare, make sure that a cryptographic hash is provided for the Component.
As a minimum expectation, declare the version string as provided by the Supplier.
The Supplier Name should be identified carefully as it is a significant contributor to achieving Component identification at scale.
The Supplier Name should be identified as the Supplier that is providing the software at the time of the SBOM creation.
As a minimum expectation, the Supplier Name should be declared for all Components.

However, the Supplier Name for supplied software should be declared differently depending on whether it was incorporated in the Primary Component unmodified or modified from how it was supplied from the upstream Supplier.

If the supplied software package or Component was unmodified when included, the Supplier Name entered is the upstream Supplier’s name with the following guidance: For Suppliers of commercially licensed software, enter the Legal Entity name.
If the Legal Entity name is not globally unique, consider adding the Supplier’s jurisdiction.
In the unlikely case that the Legal Entity name is unknown, consider using the name of the software vendor from https://nvd.nist.gov/products/cpe.
Code: Use vendor name if supplier unknown

Recommendation / Requirement: For Suppliers of open-source software, list the project name.
If known, add the host foundation before the project name (e.g., Apache Tomcat).
Referencing the open-source software (OSS) copyright statements can assist in identifying the supplier(s) or creator(s) of the software.

Although not a recommended practice, if the component’s other attributes uniquely and unambiguously identify the component and the upstream supplier is difficult to identify, either: ■ Enter the domain URL of the software and/or the namespace of the Package URL (PURL). ■ Enter supplier name as unknown.
Code: Use URL or mark supplier unknown

Recommendation / Requirement: If the supplied software package or Component was modified by the Primary Component’s Supplier before being incorporated, enter the Primary Component’s Supplier as the Supplier Name.
Code: Use primary supplier name for modified

Recommendation / Requirement: Additionally, enter the Component’s upstream Supplier in an Attribute field communicating its heritage relationship (e.g., heritage or pedigree).
It is necessary to capture the heritage relationship to enable proper vulnerability monitoring of the Component.
The Originating Supplier, if different from the upstream Supplier, may be a beneficial Attribute to capture for the Component as well.
Both [globally unique or locally unique identifiers] may be declared in the SBOM and provide value.
Minimum Expected - at least one unique identifier should be declared for each Component listed in the SBOM.
Code: Declare at least one unique identifier

Recommendation / Requirement: A globally unique identifier is preferred.
Recommended Practice - list as many globally unique identifiers as available for the Component.
Provide a hash for any Component listed in the SBOM for which the hash was provided or sufficient information is available to generate the hash.
If sufficient information is not available, indicate as unknown.
Code: Indicate hash as unknown if unavailable

Recommendation / Requirement: Along with the hash, provide the hash algorithm and the Component object being hashed to enable reproducibility.
Using a secure hash algorithm is recommended.
Provide at least one hash of the Primary Component at this maturity level.
Along with the minimally expected hash, provide the hash algorithm and the Component object being hashed to enable reproducibility.
If less cryptographically secure, hashes need to be included, adding an additional cryptographically secure hash is required.
Code: Add secure hash if weak hashes used

Recommendation / Requirement: Relationships and relationship completeness declared for the Primary Component and direct Dependencies.
Relationships and relationship completeness declared for all Included Components listed in the SBOM.
Relationships and relationship completeness to as many dynamic and remote Components as possible (e.g., loaded Components or services) are identified.
The choice of direction is not important to the model, as long as one direction is chosen and used consistently.
It is important in this example to maintain the heritage of the modified Component and convey that it has been modified.
Provide license information for the Primary Component.
If the Component’s license is not available in a standard form, refer to the specification for the SBOM format being utilized for alternate methods for declaring the license information.
Rationale/Context: If standard license identifier (e.g., SPDX) is missing, alternate methods must be used

Recommendation / Requirement: Provide license information for as many Components as possible.
Code: Provide license for as many components as possible

Recommendation / Requirement: Provide license information for all listed SBOM Components.
Attestation of Concluded License information, i.e., license text and concluded terms and conditions, is included in the SBOM.
Rationale/Context: Full license text and terms can be included for better legal clarity

Recommendation / Requirement: Provide copyright notice for the Primary Component.
Provide copyright notice for as many Components as possible.
Code: Provide copyright for as many components as possible

Recommendation / Requirement: Provide copyright notice for all listed SBOM Components.
It is expected that, if an SBOM has undeclared data, the Author continues to work to remove the barriers and reissue the SBOM with the appropriate data.
Code: Reissue SBOM when undeclared data resolved
Rationale/Context: Missing or withheld data should be addressed and a corrected SBOM issued

Recommendation / Requirement: SBOMs must handle cases of missing Attributes gracefully.

A basic recommendation is to always provide all of the Baseline Attributes but explicitly define values that differentiate between “no assertion” (i.e., data is missing) and “no value” (i.e., the Attribute is not applicable for this specific SBOM).
Code: Differentiate no assertion vs no value

Recommendation / Requirement: Alternatively, an SBOM format can permit missing Baseline Attributes and treat them as default values (i.e., “no assertion” or “no value”).
Declaring a Baseline Attribute as “no assertion” or “no value” is only used when necessary to provide an SBOM in a reasonably timely manner.
Code: Only use no assertion/value when necessary

Recommendation / Requirement: Declaring a Baseline Attribute as “no assertion” or “no value” is used rarely when sufficient effort matching the potential security risk of that Component is spent.
When a Baseline Attribute for a Component has been declared as “no assertion” or “no value”, it is proactively tracked as a compliance gap.
Code: Track no assertion/value as compliance gap

Recommendation / Requirement: Indicate the Component as redacted and provide a redaction rationale.
Remove any Component identifying information but preserve the Component’s version or cryptographic hash.
Maintain any of the dependency relationships to or from the redacted Component in a manner that honors the redaction.
A Component’s Attributes that could identify it are redacted only when a Supplier contract requires it.
The contract with a Supplier of a Component that requires redaction is approached for re-negotiation of a contract that allows Supply Chain Transparency.
Alternatively, a more transparent upstream Supplier is selected for future software development.
An SBOM system needs to be able to indicate that the list of Component Dependencies is incomplete.
Every Direct Dependency to the Primary Component is identified in the SBOM with all Baseline Attributes identified (or indicated as shown in Section 2.3.1).
Deeper Dependencies may be declared as unknown, when necessary.
Code: Mark deeper dependencies as unknown if necessary

Recommendation / Requirement: It is recommended to provide a rationale if Dependencies are declared as unknown.
Contact the upstream Supplier for their SBOM to provide the Component data needed.
Code: Obtain dependency data from upstream supplier

Recommendation / Requirement: Provide this information either nested within your Primary Component’s SBOM or separately.
Use tooling to produce SBOMs for upstream supplied software where an SBOM cannot be procured.
Also, use tooling to gather data from upstream Supplier SBOMs and your organizational development to assist in producing robust SBOMs.

Section ID & Heading: 2.4 Supplemental Information to Support Use Cases
Recommendation / Requirement: An SBOM ecosystem must support the ability to cryptographically authenticate and verify SBOM information.

Section ID & Heading: 2.4 Supplemental Information to Support Use Cases
Recommendation / Requirement: In general, this means that authors must be able to digitally sign SBOMs.

Section ID & Heading: 2.4 Supplemental Information to Support Use Cases
Recommendation / Requirement: Consumers must be able to verify signatures.
In addition to the Baseline Attributes, authors should conform to the specifications of their chosen SBOM formats.
The Supplier defines Components that the Supplier creates themselves.
The Supplier produces baseline and any supplemental Attributes for those Components.
The Supplier enumerates all directly included Components.
SBOM information will ideally be generated as an integral part of the Supplier’s software build and packaging processes.
Code: Integrate SBOM generation into build & packaging
Rationale/Context: Automating SBOM creation reduces gaps and errors

Recommendation / Requirement: Any entity creating, modifying, packaging, and delivering software or software systems is responsible for defining Components.
Any entity creating, modifying, packaging, and delivering software or software systems is responsible for creating SBOMs.
When SBOMs for Included Components are available from upstream Suppliers, those SBOMs are provided with or incorporated into the primary SBOM.
Where such information is not available, a Supplier can provide “best effort” SBOMs.
Code: Provide best-effort SBOMs when upstream missing

Recommendation / Requirement: Identity Attributes are essential for an SBOM.
An SBOM needs to be created when a Component is released.
consider using the Type Attribute of SBOM to indicate the context from which the SBOM was created or assembled.
Code: Use Type attribute for SBOM context

Recommendation / Requirement: A new SBOM should be created when the Component is updated or versioned, including when new upstream Components are added.
Code: Create new SBOM on update/version changes

Recommendation / Requirement: Ideally, changes to Components are indicated by a change in the Version String Attribute.
Maintaining a current SBOM with information and Baseline Attributes that are as complete as possible is essential.
It is necessary to exchange SBOM information.
As part of delivering the Component, the Supplier also delivers the SBOM.
They can be provided as additional files as part of a Component’s distribution or delivery.
For devices with storage and power constraints, one option is to provide a URL to look up SBOM information on a Supplier’s website.
Code: Provide SBOM URLs for constrained devices

Recommendation / Requirement: Dynamic access to an SBOM may be a good option for such devices as well.
Code: Allow dynamic SBOM access for constrained devices

Recommendation / Requirement: Suppliers create SBOMs for Components the Suppliers develop themselves.
Code: Suppliers create SBOMs for own components

Recommendation / Requirement: Suppliers define these Components.
For upstream Components, Suppliers obtain SBOMs from the appropriate upstream Suppliers.
If upstream SBOMs are not available, the Supplier or other authors can create SBOMs, even when this involves researching the appropriate entry or omitting Baseline Attributes.
Code: Create SBOMs for missing upstream info

Recommendation / Requirement: An SBOM must list a Primary Component, which defines the subject of the SBOM.
An SBOM lists Components that are: Originally created by a Supplier who is the authoritative source of the software.
An SBOM lists Components that are: Integrated as a Component from an upstream Supplier who also provides an SBOM.
An SBOM lists Components that are: Integrated as a Component from an upstream Supplier who does not provide an SBOM.
As part of delivering Components to users, Suppliers also deliver the associated SBOM(s) or provide a means for the Consumer to easily obtain SBOMs.
Suppliers are responsible for the Components they create and include in an SBOM.
Suppliers are also responsible for providing the collected set of Components to their downstream Consumers.
When a Supplier creates such SBOMs, the Supplier is expected to clearly convey that they are only the Author of the SBOM and are not the Supplier of the Component.
Code: Distinguish SBOM author vs actual supplier

Recommendation / Requirement: All Suppliers create SBOMs for their own Components.
When SBOMs for upstream Components are not available, a Supplier may need to Author an SBOM for a different Supplier’s Component.
Code: Author SBOMs for upstream components if missing

Recommendation / Requirement: Additionally, including the end-of-life date and level-of-support for the Components as supplemental to the SBOM provides the entity performing an impact assessment of a vulnerability with crucial information for mitigation options.
Code: Include EOL & support level in SBOM

Recommendation / Requirement: This use case requires associations of different licenses and types of licenses to Components and a way to evaluate the net effect of different Components with different licenses combined into an assembled product.

Secure Supply Chain of the source and integrity of Components requires information about the pedigree and provenance of Components, such as how they were built and packaged, who created and modified them, and their chain of custody through the Supply Chain.
As with the other use cases, the secure Supply Chain documentation will require additional Attributes about Components, different relationship types, and likely different Supplier information.

To be useful to end-user organizations, an SBOM needs to include baseline identity and relationship information that allows software Components to be correlated and linked as they move through the secure software Supply Chain.
